title: Radio-frequency reflectometry in silicon carbide large-area transistors
authors: Alexander Zotov, Conor McGeough, Megan Powell, Alessandro Rossi
abstract: Radio-frequency (RF) reflectometry is widely used for high-bandwidth readout of semiconductor quantum devices at cryogenic temperatures, but its application has mainly been limited to nanoscale structures with relatively small capacitances. Here, we investigate RF readout in a different regime by applying gate-based reflectometry to a large-area silicon carbide transistor with parasitic capacitances orders of magnitude larger than those of typical quantum devices, conditions normally expected to hinder RF readout. We observe a gate-dependent RF response which degrades and eventually vanishes as temperature is lowered, although MOSFET operation in DC transport is maintained down to deep cryogenic temperatures. We attribute this behaviour to impedance changes introduced by carrier freeze-out in the transistor drift region, and propose a modified circuit configuration designed to restore sensitivity under these conditions. These results establish how parasitic pathways and device geometry can limit RF readout, providing insight into the design of scalable cryogenic-CMOS quantum systems.
summary: This paper demonstrates RF reflectometry on large-area SiC transistors, showing that while the technique works well at room temperature, cryogenic cooling causes the signal to vanish due to carrier freeze-out increasing device resistance. The authors propose and validate a modified circuit design that successfully restores the RF readout sensitivity under these challenging low-temperature conditions.
main_problem: The study investigates the feasibility and limitations of using radio-frequency (RF) reflectometry to read out the electrical state of large-area silicon carbide (SiC) transistors at cryogenic temperatures.
main_result: RF readout sensitivity degrades significantly at low temperatures due to carrier freeze-out increasing the drift region resistance, but a modified circuit configuration can restore sensitivity by redirecting the RF current path.
method: The authors combine DC transport measurements with RF reflectometry using a Vector Network Analyzer (VNA) and analyze the reflection coefficient magnitude (|S11|) across varying gate voltages and temperatures.
model_or_system: The physical system is a large-area, vertical 4H-SiC MOSFET. The analysis relies on a lumped-element equivalent circuit model that incorporates device parasitics, interconnects, and temperature-dependent carrier freeze-out effects.
key_observables: Reflection coefficient magnitude (|S11|), DC transfer characteristics (Id vs Vgs), and the change in |S11| ($\Delta |S11|$).
important_parameters: Cryogenic temperature (28 K), resonant frequency ($\approx 566$ MHz), and the ratio of parasitic capacitances to device capacitances.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the RF current path is dominated by changes in the effective resistance of the drift region at low temperatures, and that parasitic elements can be accurately modeled.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic setup, the measured |S11| response vs. Vgs at room temperature, and the comparison between the original and proposed circuit configurations for restoring sensitivity.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by establishing the baseline RF readout at room temperature, demonstrating sensitivity to gate voltage. It then shows the temperature-dependent degradation of this signal, attributing it to carrier freeze-out. Finally, it proposes and validates a modified circuit design to overcome this cryogenic limitation.
why_interesting: While the focus is on solid-state electronics, the methodology of using high-frequency impedance measurements to probe subtle changes in resistive/capacitive coupling at cryogenic temperatures is relevant to characterizing quantum interfaces and superconducting circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic drawing (not to scale) of the bare-die commercial chip containing the DUT (Wolfspeed CPM2-1200- 0025A), showing the source (S) and gate (G) metal pads on the top surface; the drain (D) contact occupies the full backside of the die and is not shown. (b) Simplified PCB layout and measurement set-up for combined DC transport and RF reflectometry. The RF excitation and DC gate bias are combined through a bias tee (BT). (c) Cross-sectional schematic of the vertical MOSFET along dashed white line in panel (a), with small-signal elements overlaid, indicating the physical origin of the capacitances and resistances included in the model. Rdrift is an effective resistance that...

FIG. 2. (a) DC transfer characteristics measured at room temperature and VDS = 50 µV, showing the onset of transport through the channel at V RT th ≈1.5 V. (b) Magnitude of the measured reflection coefficient |S11| at room temperature for VGS = 0 V and VGS = 4.5 V, including repeats. (c) Frequency-dependent magnitude of the change in the reflection coefficient, |∆S11| (with offset for clarity), probing the gate effect for voltages smaller (red) and larger (orange) than threshold voltage at room temperature. Dots show experimental data and solid lines show the corresponding circuit simulations. In the simulations, the resistive elements of the DUT varied as follows: for VGS < V RT th , Rch =...

FIG. 3. (a) DC transfer characteristic measured at T = 28 K and VDS = 10 mV, showing gate-controlled turn-on and V cryo th ≈6 V. (b) Magnitude of the reflection coefficient for VGS = 0 V and VGS = 10 V, T = 28 K. (c) Frequency dependent magnitude of the change in the reflection coefficient, |∆S11| (with offset for clarity), probing the gate effect for voltages smaller (light blue) and larger (dark blue) than threshold voltage at T = 28 K. Dots show experimental data and solid lines show the corresponding circuit simulations. In the simulations, the resistive elements of the DUT varied as follows: for VGS < V cryo th , Rch = 10 MΩand 3.4 kΩ< Rdrift < 8.6 kΩ; for VGS > V cryo th , Rdrift =...

FIG. 4. (a) Schematic of the modified PCB configuration, in which additional components (pink) are introduced to redistribute the RF current through gate-dependent DUT impedances. At each of the source and drain nodes, a ca- pacitor provides a resonant RF path with Lr, while an in- ductor acts as an RF choke. (b) Simulated magnitude of the change in the reflection coefficient, |S11,i −S11,j|, for the original PCB configuration (dashed line) and the proposed configuration (solid line), evaluated for a change in channel resistance 0.2 Ω< Rch < 10 MΩ. Other parameters used in the simulations are Lr = 4 nH, CrS = CrD = 11 pF, LbS = LbD = 500 nH.

FIG. 5. Measured magnitude of the reflection coefficient |S11| at room temperature (dots) for different grounding configura- tions tested (color coded), and associated circuit simulations (solid lines). Traces are shifted vertically for clarity.
